text: all right hello everybody so this is a super fun case um i don't know how i got this lucky i was on call one night in this young twenty something year old male uh very healthy very skinny came to the er with what the uh er physician said was the worst prolapsed hemorrhoid he had ever seen and so i i came in and i found this large protruding soft mass coming out of his anus as you can see there and I reduced it in the ER and then this is a follow-up CT scan where you can see that lipoma kind of up there in the sigmoid colon. So he had this five centimeter lipoma right in the sigmoid colon. It caused complete internal interception of the sigmoid into the rectum and so After everything was said and done, and I got the colonoscopy and everything, I took him for a segmental resection.
The surgeon addresses adhesions or scar tissue between the sigmoid colon and rectum, likely due to intermittent intussusception, and begins the procedure by restoring normal anatomical alignment.
The surgeon has freed the redundant sigmoid colon, revealing a large lipoma occupying most of the colonic lumen. A tattoo is visible distal to the lipoma, marking its location.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the thin, floppy mesentery of the sigmoid colon, progressing towards the proximal resection margin and then moving downwards, facilitating a straightforward segmental resection.

So my approach to this case was a little bit different than some of my previous posts where I was using a right lower quadrant, twelve millimeter robotic port. I just did four eight millimeter ports on this as so many other people are doing because I knew that he was probably going to have very favorable anatomy to do a trans anal extraction of the specimen and then intercorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the dissection at the rectosigmoid junction, preparing to use the vessel sealer for further tissue manipulation.

some point yeah just take the vessel sealer and come across I only do about a half of a cycle before cutting I have done it without burning at all and it just causes so much oozing and bleeding that it's it's kind of frustrating so I've had good luck with this and I feel like I When you see the type of anastomosis I'm doing, I'm getting rid of that burnt tissue.
The surgeon opens the proximal colon and carefully cleans off the surrounding fat to prepare the area for placing a purse string suture.
The surgeon uses a thoracic ring forcep to extract the specimen, with a visible lipoma extruding during the process. The specimen is removed smoothly without excessive force on the rectum.
The surgeon uses two points of retraction to position the anvil stationary, then pulls the lips of the colon over it. A purse string suture is applied to secure a cuff of tissue around the anvil.

The surgeon prepares the anvil and begins placing a purse string suture around the rectum, ensuring overlap on the right side for a secure closure. The suture is cinched tightly to accommodate a 33mm EEA stapler, aiming to deploy the spike through the center of the purse string.
The surgeon operates from below and at the console to align and connect the two sections of the colon, then returns between the patient's legs to fire the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the scope reaches up to and above the anastomosis site.
